slides.heading: Those ideas you just discovered? Git already built them. 🎉
slides.body:
You didn't realize it, but in the last steps you invented version control yourself.

You figured out that you need:
✅ A way to take snapshots
✅ Labels for each snapshot
✅ A timeline to scroll through
✅ A shared place for teams

Git is a tool that does exactly these four things. Nothing more, nothing less.

Every Git command you'll ever learn maps directly to one of these ideas.
bullets: git commit → takes a labelled snapshot
git push → sends snapshots to the shared place
git pull → gets others' snapshots from the shared place

illustration.hint: Tap each idea — see the Git command that does it

items.label: Take a labelled snapshot
items.detail: git add (choose what to include) → git commit -m "your label" (take the snapshot). That's it. One snapshot taken.

items.label: See the timeline of all snapshots
items.detail: git log --oneline — shows every snapshot ever taken, newest first, each with its label. Clean, instant, searchable.

items.label: Go back to any snapshot
items.detail: git checkout <snapshot-id> — takes you back to any point in history. Your files change to match that exact moment.

items.label: Share snapshots with others (remote)
items.detail: git push — your snapshots go to GitHub. git pull — their snapshots come to you. Git merges automatically.

slides.heading: Git organises your work into three zones
slides.body:
Here's the one slightly new idea in Git — something that actually makes it *better* than just a backup tool.

Before taking a snapshot, you get to choose *exactly* what goes into it.

Imagine you're packing a bag. Your room has lots of things. You don't pack everything — you choose what's relevant for this trip.

Git works the same way:

🗄️ **Your Room** = all your files (some changed, some not)
🛏️ **Your Bag** = things you've chosen to include in the next snapshot
📸 **The Photo** = the snapshot itself, stored forever
bullets: 📁 Working Directory = your room (everything you're working on)
📋 Staging Area = your bag (what you're choosing to snapshot)
🏛️ Repository = the photo album (all your permanent snapshots)
💡 This lets you make clean, focused snapshots instead of messy all-at-once saves

illustration.hint: Git's three zones — connected to real life

left.title: Packing for a trip
revealContent: 🛏️ Clothes laid on the bed = what you've chosen
🧳 Packed suitcase = locked and ready to go
You don't pack everything — you choose what's right for this trip

right.title: Git's three zones
revealContent: 📋 Staging Area = what you've chosen for the next snapshot
Each snapshot is focused — not just 'everything I touched today'

illustration.successMessage: 🎉 Staging lets you make clean, focused snapshots instead of messy saves!

slides.heading: Why is the 'bag' step useful? 🤔
slides.body:
This is the part most tutorials skip — but it's really important.

Imagine Kavya has been working all day. She fixed a bug in the login page AND added a new feature to the dashboard.

Those are two separate things. She wants two separate snapshots:
- "Fix login bug"
- "Add dashboard feature"

With the staging area, she can take the login files and put them in the bag first. Snapshot. Then put the dashboard files in. Snapshot again.

Two clean snapshots. Not one messy "I did a bunch of stuff today" snapshot.

See the difference in the interactive demo below.
bullets: ❌ Without staging: one big messy snapshot of everything
✅ With staging: two focused snapshots that tell a clear story
📖 Your snapshot history becomes readable, like a book
🔍 Finding when something broke becomes easy

illustration.hint: See the three zones in action — click files to move them through
illustration.tip: Kavya can stage login.js → commit it → then stage dashboard.js → commit separately. Two clean snapshots.

slides.heading: Now the commands will make complete sense
slides.body:
Before you type a single command, you already understand *exactly* what Git does and why.

Let's connect everything:

**git init** — tells Git to start watching this folder
**git status** — shows what's changed and what's staged
**git add** — puts files into your bag (staging)
**git commit** — takes the snapshot of what's in your bag
**git log** — shows your timeline of all snapshots
**git branch** — creates a parallel timeline for experiments

All of these are just the ideas you already worked out. Let's go try them.
bullets: ⌨️ Now let's connect them to real commands
→ Click Complete to move to the practice steps!

illustration.hint: Watch the complete Git workflow — click through each step

steps.action: git add README.md → file moves to staging area
git commit -m 'Start project' → snapshot taken and locked
git add app.js → next file staged for the next snapshot
git commit -m 'Add task logic' → second snapshot, permanent

illustration.doneMessage: 🎉 That's the complete Git workflow. You're ready for the terminal!
